danger_signals.visual_form: Character palette shifts and redesigned sprite animations for Balrog, Vega, Sagat, M. Bison
danger_signals.meaning: Escalating threat level; these are the final boss-tier opponents with enhanced movesets and aggression

danger_signals.visual_form: Red health bar draining during combat exchanges
danger_signals.meaning: Immediate combat threat; opponent's readiness to deploy special moves increases as health lowers

danger_signals.visual_form: Visual animation changes in idle and attack poses for each fighter
danger_signals.meaning: Opponent state readiness; crouching stance indicates defensive positioning or special move charging

danger_signals.visual_form: Background elements specific to each fighter's native stage (destructible props, crowds, environmental features)
danger_signals.meaning: Arena dominance indicators; fighters perform better on their home stages visually and psychologically

safety_signals.visual_form: Full green/colored health bar at top of screen
safety_signals.meaning: Player character at peak readiness; maximum options available for offensive engagement

safety_signals.visual_form: Character in upright, balanced fighting stance with distance from opponent
safety_signals.meaning: Safe spacing; player has time to react and select next action without immediate threat

safety_signals.visual_form: Visual white flash and victory pose animation
safety_signals.meaning: Successful round completion; temporary safety before next round begins

faction_indicators.color_identity: Standard palette (Ryu blue/white, Ken white/red, Chun-Li blue, Blanka green, Guile white/blue, Dhalsim orange/brown, Zangief red, E. Honda white/blue)
faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Character-specific visual motifs (Ryu's karate gi, Ken's gi, Chun-Li's qipao, etc.)
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Alternate color palettes available when selected by opponent in mirror matches; blue name tag indicates alternate palette opponent
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Each character's home stage reflects cultural/national identity (Japan dojo, USA military base, China pagoda, Brazil jungle, USA Air Force base, India temple, USSR gymnasium, Japan sumo ring)

faction_indicators.color_identity: Darker, more menacing palettes (Balrog black/gold, Vega white/purple, Sagat orange/gold, M. Bison purple/red)
faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Visually distinct character designs indicating elite status; Balrog wears boxing attire, Vega wears claw and mask, Sagat wears eyepatch, M. Bison wears military dictator outfit
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Toned down versions as playable characters but retain intimidating visual design; retain ending sequence with sinister-sounding music and character portraits
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Grand Masters appear in later tournament stages; their presence signals narrative progression toward final conflict

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Facing Original Eight fighters with standard palettes and balanced movesets; bright, neutral stage lighting
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Opponents use predictable attack patterns; AI difficulty scales gradually; characters perform at base capability level

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Increased visual aggression in opponent animations; stage backgrounds show more crowded audiences and environmental detail
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Opponents deploy special moves more frequently; attack combinations become more complex; guard behavior tightens

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Facing visually dominant characters with intimidating designs; stage backgrounds shift to darker, more ominous palettes; character portraits change to reflect menace
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Opponents use advanced combos and special move sequencing; reduced recovery windows after attacks; aggressive pressure tactics dominate

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: One player displays standard palette, opponent displays alternate color scheme; blue name tag appears under opponent's life bar
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Character-identical movesets and speed; psychological tension created through visual differentiation only

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Bright victory flash, character performs winning pose, stage momentarily freezes
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Opponent enters knocked-down state; brief pause before round reset

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Grand Master victory sequence shows character portrait overlaid with scrolling text-specific narrative; sinister music plays; staff roll begins
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: No further opponent engagement; narrative closure through visual and audio presentation

environmental_storytelling.example: Ryu's stage is a traditional Japanese dojo, visually communicating his character as disciplined martial artist; Ken's stage is a modern American dojo with Western architectural elements, showing his American wealth and training origin

Background crowds in different stages reflect the cultural setting; Japanese stage shows traditional observers, American stages show diverse spectators, establishing geographic and cultural context without dialogue

environmental_storytelling.technique: Palette and lighting shifts across tournament progression
environmental_storytelling.example: Early rounds feature bright, neutral lighting; later Grand Master fights use darker, more dramatic lighting and background color shifts, visually communicating the stakes and danger escalation
Grand Masters display specific ending animations with character-specific portrait and narrative text, providing visual closure that differentiates their story arcs from standard fighters

environmental_storytelling.technique: Visual costume design as character archetype
environmental_storytelling.example: M. Bison's military dictator outfit, Sagat's scarred appearance and eyepatch, and Vega's masked, aristocratic design communicate their role as formidable antagonists through visual language alone

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Standard attack animations, predictable special move timing, balanced offense/defense ratio
Increased special move frequency, more aggressive pressure, less defensive spacing
Rapid dash punches, high-priority throws, sustained pressure; visually distinct boxing stance indicates grappling threat
Wall-climbing aerial attacks, claw strikes with extended range, evasive movement; white/purple palette and masked design signal mobility threat
Tiger uppercut spam, high-damage single strikes, mid-range dominance; eyepatch and scarring visually reinforce reputation as formidable opponent
Psycho Power projectiles, teleportation movement, psychic throw attacks; purple aura and military dictator design communicate otherworldly threat level
Identical moveset and speed to player character; alternate palette visual distinction allows strategic mind-game engagement without mechanical difference
Victory pose animation, bright visual flash, opponent knockdown; communicates dominance through celebratory visual language

notes: Street Fighter II' Champion Edition on Genesis (1993) communicates world-state primarily through character palette design, stage environment associations, and opponent movepool escalation. The game lacks explicit narrative dialogue but uses visual hierarchy (Grand Masters appearing in later tournaments with darker, more menacing designs) to signal progression and danger. Mirror matches introduce psychological tension through visual differentiation (alternate palette + blue name tag) while maintaining mechanical identity, creating a unique communication layer. The inclusion of Grand Masters as playable characters in Champion Edition represents a major world-state shift from the original, visually marked by their distinct character designs and dedicated ending sequences with narrative text. Stage backgrounds function as cultural/geographic markers that reinforce character identity and establish the global tournament setting. Behavioral tells rely heavily on animation design and movepool visibility rather than explicit UI warnings, requiring player pattern recognition.
